summary: DHH announces Hotwire 1.0, combining Stimulus 3 and Turbo 7 into a complete front-end framework that represents his ideal web development stack. He argues that after a decade of JavaScript framework complexity—React, Webpack, Babel—the web is swinging back toward HTML-centric approaches. Hotwire will become the default JavaScript answer in Rails 7, championing server-rendered HTML for both initial and subsequent page updates. DHH frames this as 'conceptual compression': distilling the real advances of the 2010s JavaScript era into something a single developer can understand and use. He criticizes the specialization explosion that made modern web development inaccessible to small teams and solo developers. The core philosophy is that most web apps are still CRUD operations connecting databases to the web, and the tooling should reflect that simplicity.
key_points: Hotwire 1.0 (Stimulus 3 + Turbo 7) is the complete front-end stack for Rails 7, replacing the need for React-style SPA frameworks
React's dominance has splintered into alternatives like Vue and Svelte, and ESM plus ES6 are making build-less development viable
The 2010s JavaScript complexity binge created thousands of specialized roles that locked out small teams and beginners from 'best practices'
Conceptual compression—boiling down a decade of front-end advances into simpler abstractions—is the antidote to framework fatigue
Most web development is still CRUD: connecting databases to the web through workflows, and tooling should match that reality
money_quotes: Babel me this, Webpack me that, React all the things.
We just need a few more of them to realize that HTML is not just good for the first render, it's also great for all the subsequent ones.
It's about spending some of those core advances in web technology to pay off the complexity loans we binged on during the 2010s.
We have to get back to the idea that a single developer might dare understand it all. Might do it all.
We desperately need more conceptual compression.
Fast, compelling user interfaces that let people get on with the job of... mostly creating, reading, updating, and deleting rows in a database.
Twenty-five years after I wrote my first web application, that's still mostly what we do!

Wrestling with the pendulum as it swings from conceptual expansion back to conceptual compression. Trying to turn all those real advances into something that's easier to use, more fun to work with. That's the stuff of A Life's Work.
key_insight: After a decade of JavaScript framework complexity that fragmented web development into countless specializations, the industry needs conceptual compression—and Hotwire delivers it by putting HTML back at the center of web applications.
